_change_from_v2: RESTORED POST /match-buyers and POST /notify (+ new GET /notify-status/{buyer_id}). These existed in the original division of work but were dropped in v2. They're back because Person 3's 'buyer gets notified' screen depends on them.

PRICING LOGIC PLACEMENT — DECISION MADE: pricing stays entirely in /generate-listing. /analyze-product owns condition + routing determination (including applying return_reason_score_modifier to the raw vision score). See _pricing_logic_placement_DECISION inside /generate-listing for the full reasoning.

Condition tiers and routing rules from config.json are now explicitly embedded in this contract (see shared_reference) so /analyze-product and /generate-listing's logic is fully spelled out, not just referenced.

return_reason is now OPTIONAL / nullable on /analyze-product and /generate-listing — Entry Point B (voluntary 'Give it a Second Life' listings) has no return reason. When null, return_reason_score_modifier = 0 (no adjustment).

/generate-listing response now includes entry_point ('A' = return, 'B' = voluntary listing), derived from whether return_reason was provided. This is what should be written to transactions.json as transaction_type later (A -> 'return', B -> 'second_life_listing').
/generate-listing now documents the 'Below Threshold' edge case — these items are not eligible for Second Life and the endpoint returns an error.

FIXED MOCK DATA MISMATCHES: v2's 'analyze_product_rahul_p003' mock described a 'Pigeon Baby Feeding Spoon Set' (Rs 299) that does not match products.json P003 (which is an iPhone 15 phone case, Rs 499). v2's 'analyze_product_sneha_p011' mock described resistance bands, but products.json P011 is a desk lamp — resistance bands are actually P006. Both fixed below: the voluntary-listing example now correctly uses P007 (Pikipo Baby Rattle, Rs 399, Baby Products, hub H002), and the resistance-bands example now correctly uses P006.

DEMO PRODUCT SWAP (v3 → v4): Baby monitor replaced with P009 (boAt Aavante Bar 490 Bluetooth Speaker, Rs 1499, hub H001) as Rahul's voluntary listing product. Buyer updated to Ashish Verma (U001) — perfect 100-point match (Electronics wishlist · H001 hub · within Rs 3000 limit). New mocks added: analyze_product_p009_rahul_voluntary, listing_p009_rahul_voluntary, match_buyers_p009, notify_u001, notify_status_u001_pending. /compare response_success consistency_score standardised to 91 to match demo mock. /generate-listing response_success updated: P003 example now correctly shows Amazon Basics iPhone 15 Case (Rs 499, Entry Point B) instead of stale Pigeon baby spoon set.

MOCK CLEANUP (v4): Removed stale P007/Arjun Kapoor mocks (analyze_product_p007_voluntary_listing, match_buyers_p007, notify_u005, notify_status_u005_pending) — these belonged to the baby rattle demo flow which was replaced by the P009 Bluetooth speaker / Ashish Verma flow.

shared_reference._source_of_truth: config.json is canonical. This block is duplicated here ONLY so the contract is self-contained and readable without cross-referencing another file. If config.json changes, update this block to match.

POST /analyze-product._description: Accepts product photos + product_id + optional return_reason. Returns an adjusted condition score, the resulting condition tier, routing decision, AI attributes, and AI description. Pricing is NOT calculated here.

request.images: multipart/form-data — one or more image files
request.product_id: string — must match a product_id in products.json
request.return_reason: string or null — must match a value in config.json return_reasons, OR null/absent for Entry Point B (voluntary 'Give it a Second Life' listings, which have no return reason)

_scoring_logic.step_1_raw_score: Bedrock Vision returns a raw visual condition score (0-100) based on the uploaded photos — damage, completeness, packaging, cleanliness.
_scoring_logic.step_2_adjust: adjusted_score = clamp(raw_score + return_reason_score_modifier[return_reason ?? 'null_or_absent'], 0, 100). A 'Defective' return drags the score down even if the photos look fine, because the defect may not be visible (e.g. internal electronics). A 'Wrong Size' return gets a boost because the return reason itself confirms the item was barely used.
_scoring_logic.step_3_condition: Look up adjusted_score in shared_reference.condition_tiers (score_min <= adjusted_score <= score_max) to set `condition`.
_scoring_logic.step_4_routing: Look up adjusted_score in shared_reference.routing_rules (score_min <= adjusted_score <= score_max) to set `routing`. Note routing and condition use DIFFERENT range boundaries — a score of 75 is condition 'Good' (65-84) AND routing 'second_life' (60-100); a score of 50 is condition 'Acceptable' (40-64) AND routing 'refurbishment' (40-59).
_scoring_logic.step_5_routing_reason: Generate a one-sentence explanation referencing why this routing was chosen — should be human-readable enough to read aloud to a judge.
_scoring_logic.step_6_description: Generate ai_description — 1-2 sentence visual summary of the item's condition from the photos.
_scoring_logic.worked_example: Raw vision score 73 ('Good' range on its own) + 'Defective' modifier (-20) = adjusted score 53 -> condition 'Acceptable' (40-64) AND routing 'refurbishment' (40-59), NOT 'second_life'. This is exactly why return_reason matters even when the photos alone would have qualified for Second Life.

response_success.routing_reason: Minor wear detected. Product remains fully functional and suitable for resale.
response_success.ai_description: The bottle body is in clean condition with no visible dents or cracks. The cap shows minor surface marks but seals properly.

_notes: score is the ADJUSTED score (after return_reason_score_modifier), integer 0-100
condition is exactly one of: Like New | Good | Acceptable | Below Threshold
confidence is integer 0-100, the model's confidence in the raw visual assessment (not affected by the return_reason adjustment)
routing is exactly one of: second_life | refurbishment | donation | recycling
visible_wear is exactly one of: none | minor | moderate | severe
discount_percent and discounted_price are NOT in this response — they live in /generate-listing

POST /compare._description: Accepts two sets of photos — customer photos and driver photos — and returns a fraud consistency verdict.

request.customer_images: multipart/form-data — one or more image files from customer
request.driver_images: multipart/form-data — one or more image files from driver
request.product_id: string — must match a product_id in products.json

_notes: consistency_score is integer 0-100
verdict is exactly one of: confirmed | flagged
verdict is confirmed if consistency_score >= 70, flagged otherwise
mismatch_reason is null when verdict is confirmed
mismatch_reason is a single sentence string when verdict is flagged

POST /generate-listing._description: Accepts the result of /analyze-product and returns a marketplace-ready listing with pricing. This is where ALL business rules (pricing, hub assignment, delivery estimate, entry-point classification) are applied.

_pricing_logic_placement_DECISION.decision: Pricing logic (discount_percent, discounted_price) lives ENTIRELY in /generate-listing. Nothing in /analyze-product touches price.
_pricing_logic_placement_DECISION.why: /analyze-product answers 'what condition is this item and where should it be routed' — a vision + return-context question. /generate-listing answers 'given that condition, what does the marketplace listing look like' — a pure lookup/pricing question over condition_tiers, products.json, and hubs.json. Pricing never needs the photos or another model call.
_pricing_logic_placement_DECISION.what_this_means_for_implementation: Person 1 can build /generate-listing as a pure function (condition string + product_id in, full listing JSON out) with ZERO AI dependency. It can be built and fully unit-tested before the Bedrock integration for /analyze-product is even working. This de-risks the critical path: if the Bedrock call is running late on integration day, /generate-listing is already done and waiting.

request.product_id: string — must match a product_id in products.json
request.condition: string — Like New | Good | Acceptable | Below Threshold
request.return_reason: string or null — pass through from /analyze-product request. null/absent = Entry Point B (voluntary listing)

_pricing_calculation.step_1_eligibility: If condition == 'Below Threshold' (or routing != 'second_life'), return response_error_not_eligible below — this item does not get a Second Life listing.
_pricing_calculation.step_3_price: discounted_price = round(original_price * (1 - discount_percent / 100)) — round half up. original_price comes from products.json[product_id].original_price
_pricing_calculation.step_5_delivery: estimated_delivery = 'Same Day' if the buyer's pincode is in hubs.json[hub_id].coverage_pincodes, else 'Next Day'. For the demo, default to 'Same Day' — all demo products/buyers are within their assigned hub's coverage.
_pricing_calculation.step_6_entry_point: entry_point = 'A' if return_reason is non-null, else 'B'. This single field is what later gets written to transactions.json as transaction_type ('A' -> 'return', 'B' -> 'second_life_listing').

listing.listing_title: Amazon Basics iPhone 15 Compatible Phone Case — Like New
listing.ai_description: The case is in pristine condition with no scratches or marks. Original packaging is intact with no damage.

response_error_not_eligible.error: Item condition 'Below Threshold' is not eligible for a Second Life listing. Routed to: {routing}.

_notes: listing_title is always: [product name] — [condition_badge]
discount_percent is the condition tier's discount_midpoint, no per-item variation in the prototype (see config.json _future_considerations for the dynamic-discount idea, explicitly deferred)
return_reason_display is null when return_reason was null (Entry Point B) — frontend should show 'Listed by Owner' instead of a return-reason chip in that case
amazon_verified is always true for any listing that reaches this endpoint
hub_id is pulled from products.json for the given product_id
entry_point is 'A' (return) or 'B' (voluntary listing) — drives transaction_type for the business impact dashboard

POST /match-buyers._description: Takes a newly generated listing and ranks all buyers (users.json) by how well it matches their profile. Pure Python weighted scoring — no ML, fully explainable to judges.
POST /match-buyers._restored_in_v3: Present in the original division of work but missing from v2. Restored because Person 3's notification screen depends on it.

_scoring_logic.hub_match: +35 if buyer.hub_id == `hub_id` (same fulfillment hub, displayed to the buyer as 'near you')
_scoring_logic.match_reason_template: Join only the clauses for criteria that actually matched, in this order: '[category] on your wishlist' · 'available near you' · 'within your price range'. Separator: ' · '. A buyer with all three is a 100-point full match; a buyer with zero matching criteria is excluded entirely (not returned).

matches.match_reason: Electronics on your wishlist · available near you · within your price range

_notes: matches is sorted descending by match_score
Only buyers with match_score > 0 are included

If matches is empty, the listing still goes live in the marketplace — it just isn't proactively pushed to anyone. This is fine and worth saying out loud to judges: matching is additive, not a gatekeeper.

POST /notify._description: Marks the top-ranked buyer from /match-buyers as having a pending notification. No real push infrastructure — sets an in-memory/JSON flag that the buyer's frontend polls.
POST /notify._restored_in_v3: Present in the original division of work but missing from v2. Restored because Person 3's notification banner depends on it.

request.listing: object — the full listing object from /generate-listing's response.listing

notification.listing_title: boAt Aavante Bar 490 Bluetooth Speaker — Like New

_notes: Once delivered to the frontend, the backend can clear the flag so the same notification doesn't fire repeatedly — or leave it set and let the frontend track 'already shown' client-side. Either is fine for a 36-hour build; document whichever Person 1 picks.

mock_responses._comment: Person 2 and Person 3 hardcode these during independent development, then swap for real fetch() calls at integration hour. All examples below use REAL product_ids, categories, prices, and hub_ids from products.json / users.json / hubs.json — no invented products.
